Week of 9/2-9/5

Weekly Market Summary

By Noah Halfon

Global Weekly Market Movements

U.S. Equities

  • S&P 500 Index: 6,481.50 (-0.12%)
    • SPX slipped lower this week following weaker than expected labor data and concerns over a slowing US economy. XLE energy led the decline falling -3.35% as OPEC+ agrees to boost production, causing red across the sector. XLC communication services led the green at 2.96%, with notable movements from Google.
    • The index also had a rebalance Friday, adding Applovin (APP), Emcor Group (EME), and my personal favorite: Robinhood Markets (HOOD) 107.33 (7.06% overnight → 485.91% YoY)
  • NASDAQ Composite: 21,700.39 (0.32%)
  • Dow Jones Industrial Average: 45,400.86 (-0.42%)
  • Russell 3000 Index: 3,695.84 (-0.06%)

Big Movers of the Week

    • Lululemon Athletica Inc (LULU): 167.80 (-17.99%)
      • Lululemon, the popular apparel brand has fallen again after slashing its full year outlook and posting slower sales growth. Crediting tariff impacts, comparable sales in America are down 4% while the company has reduced gross profit expectations by $240 million. Analysts predict more struggle ahead as competition and uncertainty ramp up. Almost time to buy?
    • Advances Micro Devices Inc (AMD): 151.14 (-9.39%)
      • AMD shares fell as around 6% Friday as Seaport Research downgraded the company from BUY to HOLD. Pointing to slowing demand and adoption for AMD’s AI products, the company lost more than $10 billion in market value.
    • Alphabet Inc (GOOG): 235.17 (11.29%)
      • Google shares soar after a federal judge ruled that the company will avoid penalties and will retain its chrome browser in monopoly ruling. After this, Apple and Google reached a deal to have Gemini (Google's AI chatbot) power a new Siri platform as reports say Apple will launch “AI search” inside of Siri in the next year.
    • Broadcom Inc (AVGO): 334.89 (9.18%)
      • Broadcom saw shares surge after the chipmaker reported better sales and profit for the quarter, indicating AI chip competition. FY26 growth is now set to 110% YoY, rather than the 55-60% YoY prior. Broadcom also reported it secured $10 billion in purchases from OpenAI, as their new AI chip (XPU) is supposedly an industry first. Shares of rivals NVIDIA and AMD both fell following this news.

Chinese Equities - Shanghai Composite (SHCOMP): 3,812.51 (-1.48%)

Hong Kong Equities - Hang Seng Index (HSI): 25,417.98 (-0.35%)

Japanese Equities- Nikkei 225 (NI225): 43,018.75 (1.55%)

European Equities

  • UK Index (UKX): 92,208.21 (0.23%)
  • German Index (DAX): 23,596.98 (-1.73%)

Commodities

  • Gold Futures: 3,639.80 (4.89%)
  • Crude Oil Futures: 61.27 (-3.10%)

Crypto

  • Bitcoin (BTC): 111.237.05 (0.01%)
  • Ethereum (ETH): 4,293.93 (-0.75%)

Global Macroeconomic News

Jobless Claims: Applications for US unemployment benefits have risen to its highest level since June, increasing by 8,000 to 237,000 vs the 230,000 expected. With the unemployment rate ticking up to 4.3%, markets rose taking this news as a positive, reinforcing expectations of a rate cut to support the labor market.

Nonfarm Payrolls: US nonfarm payrolls also came in weak at +22K instead of the +75K expected. Besides this, June job growth was revised down to a loss of 13,000, marking the first monthly decline since December 2020. With job growth slowing alongside a stalling wage growth, only rising 0.3% for all private nonfarm workers, a September cut appears to be secured. The probability of a 50 basis point cut has begun and has risen to 11% as markets continue to price in heavier and more cuts later in 2025.

Oil Hikes: OPEC+ agrees to boost oil production in October to help regain market share. An October increase will put OPEC+ on pace to undo the 1.66 million barrels a day of cuts that were scheduled to remain in place until the end of 2026. This move also illustrates OPECs priority to market share even while risking lower prices that pressures nations reliant on high costs. For the oil market, this could be the start of a more fragile supply environment, one more vulnerable to unforeseen supply shocks. After this announcement, the energy sector saw losses with stocks dropping across the board.

Plastics & Seasonality Trade: As oil prices soften, companies and manufacturers dependent on plastic production are likely to benefit from reduced input costs. Oil is a key input of plastic manufacturing, linking the two as well as offering price fluctuations to consumers and businesses. To focus on a more specific sector, the holiday season nears, where a reduction in energy and materials costs could help boost margins for toy manufacturers and position them to take advantage of seasonality gains. Historically, Q4 accounts for the majority of sales and profits for toy manufacturers, but inflation risks and uncertain consumer sentiment may still pose threats to increased holiday spending.

Potentially Impacted Stocks: JAKK, HAS, XOM, AMCR

Bond Market: The 10-year Treasury yield dropped to its lowest level since April after this week's labor data and continuation of stronger rate cut expectations. As traders buy to try and lock in the higher yields, the 10-year fell below 4.1% this week, while the 2-year also reached a 5 month low falling 6 basis points. Reflecting the expectations for slower economic growth, the yield curve has steepened, benefiting lenders who borrow short and lend long term debt with better rates.

Citi-Blackrock: Citi is closing down its last in house asset management arm and handing BlackRock control of roughly $80 billion in client assets. This move expands an existing partnership in which BlackRock already manages a portion of Citi’s $635 billion wealth portfolio. Marking a new movement by Citi to streamline its wealth management strategy, these moves come in hope of gaining a greater share of wealthy wallets. As Blackrock’s AUM continues to grow, it seems to be becoming more and more of the preferred manager for global banks.

Sources: Google Finance, Market Watch, CME Fedwatch, Yahoo Finance, Reuters, New York Times, Bloomberg, Wall St Journal, US Department of the Treasury, GuruFocus, Edward Jones, CNBC

Noah Halfon
